skill.name: azure-resource-manager-postgresql-dotnet
skill.description: Guides Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server management with the Azure Resource Manager SDK for .NET.
skill.summary: Manage Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server resources with .NET ARM SDK examples.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but most are false positives from markdown code fences, inline code, and documentation links. The confirmed risks are publishable warnings: sample password and TLS guidance, plus a broad Azure firewall example that users should review before production use.

medium_findings.title: Insecure Credential and TLS Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes sample administrator passwords and a connection string that sets Trust Server Certificate=true. Users may copy this into production and weaken credential handling or TLS validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are documentation examples, not executable code, but they directly show password literals and relaxed certificate validation.

medium_findings.title: Broad Azure Firewall Rule Example
medium_findings.description: The firewall example allows Azure services by using 0.0.0.0 and a broad rule name. This is legitimate Azure guidance in some cases, but it can increase database exposure if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is a documented Azure pattern, but the semantic effect is broad network access for Azure services.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Static command findings come from markdown code fences, inline type names, and package install examples. I found no skill script that runs shell commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is fenced markdown or inline markdown. There is no Ruby code, shell interpolation, or executable file in the reviewed skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Filesystem Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography matches are ordinary PostgreSQL and high availability text. The hidden file finding is the .default suffix in an Azure token scope URL, not file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding lines are prose, tables, and an Azure authentication resource string. They do not show cryptographic code or filesystem access.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Vendor Documentation and Token Scope References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Microsoft, NuGet, GitHub documentation, and an Azure token resource scope. I found no data exfiltration endpoint or unauthorized network call.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible reference links or an Azure authentication audience. There is no code that sends local data to those URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Insecure Credential and TLS Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes sample administrator passwords and a connection string that sets Trust Server Certificate=true. Users may copy this into production and weaken credential handling or TLS validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines are documentation examples, not executable code, but they directly show password literals and relaxed certificate validation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Azure Firewall Rule Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The firewall example allows Azure services by using 0.0.0.0 and a broad rule name. This is legitimate Azure guidance in some cases, but it can increase database exposure if copied without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code is a documented Azure pattern, but the semantic effect is broad network access for Azure services.

content.value_statement: Azure PostgreSQL administration requires many ARM SDK types and deployment choices. This skill provides focused .NET examples for servers, databases, backups, firewall rules, and high availability.
actual_capabilities: Shows package installation for Azure.ResourceManager.PostgreSql and Azure.Identity.
Provides C# examples for creating PostgreSQL Flexible Server resources.
Explains database, firewall rule, backup, restore, and replica workflows.
Lists key SDK types used for PostgreSQL Flexible Server management.
Includes guidance for Entra ID authentication and server configuration updates.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not run Azure commands by itself.
Examples use placeholder names, passwords, tenant IDs, and resource groups.
Users must verify current Azure SDK versions and service limits.
Production networking and authentication choices need security review.

use_cases.title: Provision a Managed PostgreSQL Server
use_cases.description: Create a Flexible Server with storage, backup, high availability, and authentication settings using C#.

use_cases.title: Review Database Operations Code
use_cases.description: Check ARM SDK usage for database creation, configuration updates, backups, restores, replicas, and scaling.

use_cases.title: Plan Secure Access Controls
use_cases.description: Compare firewall, Entra ID administrator, and connection string patterns before deploying database access changes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Server
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft C# code that creates an Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server in my resource group.

prompt_templates.title: Add Database and Firewall Rules
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for application database setup.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a database and restricted firewall rule for my application server IP range.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Production Reliability
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced setup for backup, high availability, and read replica planning.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design PostgreSQL Flexible Server settings for backups, zone redundant high availability, and a read replica.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Existing ARM SDK Code
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of security, operations, and maintainability risks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my Azure PostgreSQL ARM SDK code for risky firewall, credential, backup, and deletion patterns.

output_examples.input: Create a PostgreSQL Flexible Server with production settings.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan covering SKU, storage, backup retention, high availability, authentication, and required Azure SDK types.
A security-focused checklist that compares specific IP rules, Azure services access, and private endpoint options.

output_examples.input: Explain how to restore a database from backup.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step explanation of point-in-time restore concepts, required source server data, and naming considerations.

best_practices: Use managed identity or DefaultAzureCredential instead of storing secrets in source code.
Restrict firewall rules to known networks or private endpoints whenever possible.
Validate destructive operations, restore settings, and service limits before applying changes.
anti_patterns: Copying placeholder passwords or connection strings into production code.
Using broad 0.0.0.0 firewall rules without a documented exception.
Deleting or scaling servers without backup, approval, and rollback planning.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy Azure resources automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and C# examples for the Azure Resource Manager SDK.

faq.question: Which Azure PostgreSQL service does it cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server, not the retired Single Server service.

faq.question: Can it help with Entra ID authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for configuring an Entra ID administrator and requesting an access token.

faq.question: Are the sample passwords safe to reuse?
faq.answer: No. Replace all placeholder passwords with secrets from an approved secret manager.

faq.question: Does it include backup and restore guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows backup listing and point-in-time restore concepts for Flexible Server.

faq.question: What should I review before production use?
faq.answer: Review firewall scope, authentication mode, backup retention, high availability, and deletion safeguards.
